sentences.text: Yes.
Yes.
J-A-N-I-C-K-A.
That's her-- that's her-- that's her--
Last name, yeah.
M-I-C-H-A-L-I-N-A.
Michalina.
Michalina.
You will say, 'Michelle-ina.'
No.
It's a C-H.
And I have her address, naturally.
I have her picture here.
Oh, no.
That's not-- no, no.
She's not there anymore.
No.
They burned the whole-- the whole village, the Ukrainians, I told you that.
I assume, yes.
Everything was liquidated.
There were no other-- no Jewish people left in the ghetto.
I just, right.
I just find out a lot about them in this book.
Yeah.
That put together in Israel.
My friend-- my friend send me the book.
I have no-- This is in Hebrew.
Maybe you want to read it?
About my town.
All the pictures, and all the people I know, everybody, everything of the size of the ghetto, the size of town, everything.
[HEBREW]
[HEBREW] It looked like [HEBREW] and the-- the-- [HEBREW] one who--
Wrote the book.
Yeah.
My friend, Mikhal.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's right.
No idea where I'm going, if I'm going to get there.
I mean the four of us, that's not only me.
Then we separate with the boys too, at the end.
But it's going to-- this is some days--
From where?
No.
Just whatever I had on me.
Almost, if not we slept in the fields outside.
We-- we were still going around for-- begging for food.
We never had a bath.
We never had-- washed our face.
We never had nothing.
Only with water from--
Everything in the field.
We had no home.
We were homeless.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah, I told you, the Polish in the wintertime, yeah.
Yeah, I told you before.
That when it came-- cold weather, the-- my Polish family took me in, and not only me, sometimes-- sometimes with my friend, girlfriend, but not really the boys.
And so they--
The boys-- the boys was a problem.
Because there was-- they were Jewish-looking.
They were-- I didn't look Jewish, let's say.
I don't know.
But the boys was a little problem that they didn't want the boys in the house.
It was-- they had two kids of their own.
They had two children.
But the older son is-- was a year younger than I.
And the younger one was about two or three years older-- younger than I.
About-- when we go into forest, 14 was almost also.
That's exactly what I was going to say, yeah.
Yeah, because with them, they-- they really, thanks to them I survived.
No.
While-- while were were in the village, we were-- we were going around, you know.
But what happened during the wintertime, the Polish people were fighting with the Ukrainians.
The reason why-- I-- I-- actually it's the other way around.
The-- the Ukrainian people always wanted to have their own Ukraine.
And they never really-- never could really get it.
So since-- so the German people told the soldiers, they told them, if you help since we got rid of the Jews now, if you'll help us get rid of the Polacks, then it's gonna be your Ukraine.
They called-- that time that was the name what the fighters, Ukrainian fighters, was [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
Right.
They're called the [NON-ENGLISH], yeah.
And so finally, they were fighting for a couple of days, like eight days or so.
I was with this Polish family hiding in the same area.
So were the other girl, but on her own.
But the boys, I didn't see.
Because they were already, I think, they-- they teamed up at that time already, I think, with the partisans.
Because there were also partisans in the area.
I don't know.
Jewish or non-Jewish.
I was not there.
Because my Polish lady said to me, you can't go to partisans.
Because you're a girl.
You're a young girl.
You know what partisans can do to-- to girls.
So you go with me.
Where we go, you go.
Yeah.
So what happened, we all had to escape.
And we escaped to another town, to a bigger town that's like a-- was like a-- like--
A resort sort of like.
It was like they had the water.
I forgot.
I forgot.
So, she said, you come with us.
So I said, so I went with them.
And then we stayed there not too long.
We all had no homes anymore.
The Polacks--
No.
Her house was burned.
Everything was burned.
So, and there was no work in those families.
So we-- they decided to go-- the German people at that time, needed people to come to Germany to work on the farms and things.
Because most of the people, all the young people were in the army.
So they decided to go to Germany.
And they said to me, you coming with us.
I said, come on.
The minute I'll see a German soldier, they'll recognize I'm Jewish.
And what's gonna be with you?
Never mind me, but what's going to be with you?
And she said to me, don't worry.
And I went with her, with them.
And I went with them.
She said, we're not going to leave you here.
And we came to Germany.
We had to go through a inspection, you know, the doctor inspection.
You know, they checked us if we're healthy or not.
And--
Yeah.
She gave me her maiden name.
Which was-- it was-- Henya [?
Shalakowska. ?] [?
Geneveva ?] [?
Shalakowska. ?] So we gave the name.
And she told me that if-- I should say that she-- I'm her cousin.
And I lost my-- I was an only child.
And I have-- lost my-- during the war, I lost-- my father died and my-- my mother died during the war.
And I have nobody, except them.
And that was-- that's when we got papers that made out my passport, or whatever.
They never-- they never gave us any papers.
Because this-- then a day later, after this, a day later, the farmers came.
And they were trying to pick people to-- for their farm, to work, to live with them.
German.
Yeah.
But now, Scheidemuhl-- did I say Scheidemuhl?
We got to Schneidemuhl.
Yeah.
Yeah, we went-- with-- with the train.
I think about two, three days or so.
I suppose so.
No. it was only Polish people.
There were no Jews, except me.
But they don't know.
I'm not Jewish.
Not there, not on the train, nothing, no.
No, no.
No, I saw the other girl before we started-- before we went in, depart to Germany.
We had a stop in Rovno, that city.
In Poland, and they stayed there a few days or so.
And they took us to work, the young people only.
I guess the Germans or the Poles, I don't really know.
And then we-- they put us on a truck, regular trucks.
And then I got on the truck, I saw this girl sitting.
But we never exchanged a word with each other.
As a matter of fact, there was another one from my town sitting, older than me.
Never said one word, like we never knew each other.
Pardon?
For safety reasons, because if not, if I would start talking-- I mean, our Polish was good.
But we still had a slight accent.
But we couldn't-- we couldn't open our mouth-- my mouth, you know?
Or she couldn't open her mouth too.
And we-- and we were afraid they should catch on.
So we didn't speak to each other, and the same thing when they took us back.
We didn't-- they dropped us off.
They dropped everybody who was picked up from-- from, you know, were dropped off at the same spot.
So we went to-- to they dropped me off where they picked me up, to where the Polish family stayed.
And that was only like a couple days.
And then from there, we went to Schneidemuhl.
I went with the Polish family--
Schneidemuhl by train, and then when we get there, the same thing.
They started to-- they-- we went through this, like I said before, we went through the doctors and this or checkup.
So they checked us out, if we're healthy.
And because if you're not healthy, they really didn't want you there.
They want people to-- to go to work.
And what-- well, the next morning, they told us to line up, who are all the newcomers.
And the people came from the-- the-- the farmers.
And they were picking up families.
And my, the family, was a healthy-- healthy young people that-- the parents was still very young.
They had the children, two children, that they were able-- that could, the older son could work.
I could work.
So they took us immediately, and they send us to this gutsverwaltung.
That was a huge farm.
It was--
Yeah, that was in German it was gutsverwaltung.
Something--
Gutsverwaltung, and-- and that was managed by German people.
Well he was, but he stayed there-- everyone, I think, was supposed-- was supposed to-- had to be a Nazi.
No, I-- you know, I have the picture, you know.
Anyway, so they took us to the gutsverwaltung.
This was located between-- in a small area town, between Deutsch Krone, which was-- well let's say, and Schneidemuhl.
Schneidemuhl was the biggest city, and Deutsch Krone was a smaller town.
It was closer to the farm too.
Deutsch Krone, yeah.
And but we stayed-- the place where we stayed was-- was-- [INAUDIBLE].
I forgot already.
It was a name that the farm, you know-- Buchenwald?
No, not Buchenwald.
I forgot.
Maybe it will come to me.
Yeah.
So--
Well, beginning I did what all, everybody did.
Like the Polish people, they were also war prisoners there, Ukrainian.
Everybody worked on the farm, in the field, everybody, even Germans.
Harvesting, weeding, whatever had to be-- pick potatoes, whatever it was we had to do.
And then the-- even the Germans that were still at home, the kids, they had to work too.
And then-- then they took all these children, the young-- young men and women to the army.
At the end of the war was this already.
The end-- the end of war, we liberated '45, yeah, maybe '46, maybe.
From starting from Wirke till-- from the Poland, it was probably was like four or five years.
Then we lived together after the war a little.
Yeah, we did have Christmas naturally.
And I celebrate their holidays.
I did not celebrate my holidays.
Because then I wouldn't be-- then I became Polish.
As soon as we checked into Scheidemuhl-- my name was not-- it was no more-- nobody knew I'm Jewish, except my family.
I was Polish.
And I-- that's why I had my name, [?
Shalakowska, ?] and all this.
Henya.
Henya.
Yeah.
I felt very normal, very-- very normal.
At home and by myself.
And-- and-- when I go-- went to sleep or so.
But nothing, I had Polish friends.
Even a boyfriend, Polish.
I got a little older.
Everybody, we were-- Polish family was like one Polish family.
At that time, I started to feel I'm Polish.
No.
This is another story.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we were liberated, I said to my Polish lady, we went back to-- first we ran away.
Because we-- when we're-- we were liberated by the Russian army.
When the Russians were coming closer to us, we had to-- they made us to go further.
Away from-- away from town.
And we stopped somewhere on a-- in a different area.
In Germany, yeah.
Against the Germans, yeah.
So when they came and we had-- we went-- we went further, further where the Germans were still occupied-- which was still occupied by Germans.
Then we stopped.
And that's where-- and then I forgot about about [INAUDIBLE], where we were liberated.
Anyway, now-- then after liberation, also we had which I didn't tell you.
At the end-- when-- I didn't tell you the story that I came--
After them-- they started to-- all-- all the German youngsters went to the army.
She lost her maid, see, the lady of the house.
So from all the people--
The-- the German, now I'm talking the German.
She lost her maid, her help.
So she picked me to be the maid.
She know from that I'm from the Polish family.
I mean we worked there.
No.
At the field-- on the field.
But this was--
In her field, yeah.
However she managed the [NON-ENGLISH].
So-- so she decided to take me in.
And I was-- I was sleeping there.
Next door, next house.
They was-- they were there.
They still worked on the farm.
But I worked-- I think the reason she took me in is I used to speak Yiddish.
And don't forget, Yiddish and German is very close.
So I learned how to speak the German.
The Polish people could never-- the youngsters learned a little bit, but not the middle-aged or so could not speak any other language.
German, no.
They spoke a little, maybe Ukrainian, because it's also very close.
So-- so I was-- I was translating the-- now let's say all the mail from all the people, the Ukrainian, Polish, came into the main house where I worked.
And he, the man came, and everything came into this office.
And he told me which-- this letter came to this one.
This one came to them, or vice versa.
They came in for the letters.
And everything that had to be, they wanted to know the Polish people or the Ukrainian people, they had to use me.
[NON-ENGLISH], no.
[INAUDIBLE], yeah Wait, [INAUDIBLE], ooh.
I cannot remember.
I have to think again.
Because I had a friend, [INAUDIBLE], I might mix them up.
He was a very nice guy, very nice.
At the end-- he-- I can say he had to go to the army too.
It was to the last-- maybe-- the Russians was already coming.
They took him.
He-- he wore a-- I saw him in a uniform, not all the time, but specially when they used to have friends, invited the Nazi friends to their house for dinner, holidays.
I served them.
I was not allowed to hear.
I didn't know anything about the war.
I didn't know if there are any Jewish people left.
After the war, I thought I'm the only one who survived.
And then I was thinking, what will I do?
To be only one survivor of Jewish survivor in the whole Europe, Europe.
But thank god, that more people survived.
So-- so this was a day even he left to the army.
Then she was left with me.
That was his wife, yeah.
And she said to me-- they not-- had no children.
And she said to me, you know, I would like to adopt you as my daughter.
No idea, not to my--
Yeah.
I came with the family, and I'm the cousin.
I said, no.
Sorry.
I came with my family.
I got to go back with my family.
Wherever we go, I want to be with my family.
Because she's left-- Around the same-- it's only months of time.
Like 14--
After, yeah, yes, yes.
No, about 15.
No, when I-- 18, I was not quite 18 when I was-- after the war, when the war was over.
I married my husband when I was like-- I was [INAUDIBLE].
I was-- I needed somebody to sign for me.
I was still not fully-- like a minor.
Anyway, where was I?
So, yeah.
Well, she was left.
And the-- the Russian army was coming near closer, nearer and nearer to us.
So she came from Berlin.
And she want to-- she packed everything up.
And she was going to Berlin.
And that's when she wanted me to with her.
And she-- she wanted me to be-- she wanted me to be her daughter.
And that's when I said, no.
And we, also all the Polacks and Russian, we-- we had to also go further up, run away from the-- from the Russian army, until there was a stop, finished.
And that's where I was-- we were liberated, in [INAUDIBLE], or something like this, a town.
Germany, I'm talking about Germany now.
I'm not talking about Polish.
Yeah, everybody had to.
Because they didn't-- they didn't want us to be-- to be liberated with the-- with the Russians.
They did-- I assume they probably thought, the German people, that they're not losing the war.
That this is gonna come back, you know, it's just temporarily.
But then it-- we know in the end what it was.
So then-- so I said, no.
And she left by herself.
And I went with my family.
And that's where we-- we was-- it was [NON-ENGLISH], or so, the name of the town.
We stopped there for another couple of weeks or so, maybe less.
And then the Russians came, and we were liberated.
So then the war was over?
The war was over.
It was in April.
Yeah, yeah.
Then we were-- I went back to the Polish family, back to the farm.
Yeah, who was occupied already by the Russian soldiers.
And we was-- stayed there for a while.
And then we moved to another farm, which was close by.
It was a smaller farm.
And finally I started to see-- and there was-- there was at that time, was the Polish family and me, and the children.
Nothing happened to any of us.
But during the time when we were traveling, I don't remember-- and we were going also-- I don't know how we come-- we went through Berlin.
Whose husband?
With the German-- the Polish family.
Yeah, yeah.
When we were going, by the time-- I don't know how this can be-- back to the farm-- we went to the Scheidemuhl.
I know we went through the Berlin, and we saw Berlin on fire.
And we saw-- we went through the woods, wooded area, we also-- I think it was Berlin.
And it was also a lot, a lot of, killed there from the soldiers.
We was almost walking on the bodies.
From the German Army and the Russian Army, both, because they were fighting for every-- every step.
So it took a long-- took a while until-- until we were liberated, until we got to get liberated.
But then after, when we were liberated, we went back to the-- to the farm.
And then finally, after a while, they were all Russian, occupied Russian soldiers.
Well, the minute we were liberated, we were still in-- we also were hiding in the forest because of these airplanes that, you know, are shooting and things like that.
I said, oh my god.
I'm liberated?
And what about my family?
Well, it was because the Russian soldiers came in, and they came to start to speak Russian.
And they said, the war is over.
The war is over.
So we came out from the-- forest.
And I said, oh my god, oh myself.
What about my family?
Did they survive or they didn't?
I didn't know anything about it.
And where will I find Jewish people?
I mean, here where I am now, there's no Jewish people.
So-- but anyway, that's all-- that was the first thing that came in my mind.
And then we were moved back to-- we went-- I went-- wherever the family went, I went.
I was with them.
And then finally, they left.
I said to them, you know, I'd like to go to the cities, Polish bigger cities.
And look, maybe I can find, maybe if there are any Jewish people.
They was like, from-- since Germany-- Scheidemuhl became Poland.
After the Russian occupation, they gave it to the Polacks.
It became Polish.
Because the area where I came from, when I-- I was born, was the Russian.
Was no more Polish Ukraine.
It was Russian Ukraine or whatever.
So they gave them the Scheidemuhl that they named it Pila.
Schneidemuhl is like, is--
It's not-- no you cut something.
And Pila is also, you-- it's like when you when you have-- you will have this-- what do you call-- you cut, cut.
You cut, you cut wood or sawing you,
Right.
Right, right, right.
So--
Oh, I didn't-- That was a long story.
Yeah, I hope it's going to-- am finishing already.
